title: The perfect guitar solo. Power and emotion. An elegant balance of melody & flash.
description:
The perfect guitar solo. Power and emotion. An elegant balance of melody & flash. 

I like the way it takes you on a journey, and surprises you along the way. It plays off the chord changes nicely, accentuating the chord tones with a casual ease, even over the last few chords that shift out of key.

As usual, a sixpence is vital to get that nice metallic ping out of the string. The rest of the tone signal chain will sound dull if you just play it with an ordinary plastic plectrum. The devil's in

title: Did you know the beat of this song was recorded in a church hall? The band recorded themselves stomping & clapping, and
description:
Did you know the beat of this song was recorded in a church hall? The band recorded themselves stomping & clapping, and overdubbed it repeatedly to create the iconic thunderous rhythm.

Designed for crowd singalongs (they nailed that one!) it was intended to be percussion & vocals only. Brian May only added the solo as an after-thought, figuring it would be too "short and weird" without any guitar.

This song is one of those that feels like it was just always there, constantly being referenced a

title: Mark Knopfler demonstrating his approach to playing “Money for Nothing”. He wrote the song after overhearing a delivery
description:
Mark Knopfler demonstrating his approach to playing “Money for Nothing”. He wrote the song after overhearing a delivery man in a New York department store complain about his job. Many of the lyrics were things they said. Stings record label wanted a song righting credit so they could earn royalties. They said it sounded like “Don’t Stand So Close To Me”. 

#markknopfler #direstraits #moneyfornothing #sting #police #rockandroll #classicrock

title: Need to adjust the action on your electric guitar? Here’s how to measure.
description:
Need to adjust the action on your electric guitar? Here’s how to measure.
 
1. Start by determining if you need to adjust your truss rod. 
2. Adjust as needed in small increments. 
3. Holding the strings down at the first fret, measure your action. 
4. Start with the low E then the high E. 
 
Save this video for the next time you need to measure the action on your electric guitar. For the full video, check out the link in our bio.

title: Not the typical "Daughter" song 👧
description:
Not the typical "Daughter" song 👧

You’d think a song called "Daughter" would be a sweet tribute, but Pearl Jam went the opposite way.

It’s a pretty tragic look at a child struggling with dyslexia and a family that just sees her as "difficult." It turned a personal struggle into a global anthem for anyone who ever felt like they didn't fit in.

It’s crazy how a simple acoustic riff can carry so much weight. It’s easily one of my favorite tracks to play when I’m just messing around on the guitar

title: How to Play BRING IT ON HOME by Led Zeppelin
description:
How to Play BRING IT ON HOME by Led Zeppelin

This riff from Bring It On Home is a great example of how Jimmy Page blended blues vocabulary with hard rock.

Most of the riff sits right inside the minor pentatonic scale, but Page sneaks in one important extra note—the major third on the first fret of the G string. That little note creates tension against the bluesy sound and gives the riff its signature bite.

That’s really the secret behind a lot of Zeppelin riffs—familiar blues language, with j

title: If there is one thing to take away from Jimi Hendrix's playing, it's his method implying chords through rhythmic and mel
description:
If there is one thing to take away from Jimi Hendrix's playing, it's his method implying chords through rhythmic and melodically satisfying licks. In this mini tutorial, I'll break down a sweet phrase that you can use to outline all major triads. Let's get started! -Rob

title: Is this Jimi's best rhythm? In this excerpt from my course, I'll show...
description: Is this Jimi's best rhythm? In this excerpt from my course, I'll show you how to play the signature Hendrix rhythm from the song "Hear My Train a Comin'". It's such a cool rhythm, and definitely one of my favorites. My course, "Jimi's Influence: Hear My Train," will help you play like Jimi on this song. You'll learn an extended-length solo note-for-note, with tablature and a backing track. Check out the course here: https://texasbluesalley.com/woodshed/courses/jimis-influence-hear-my-train #blue

title: I love split triads, so when I saw this one from @rickbeato1 I had to try it. EDIT: A bunch of you a
description:
I love split triads, so when I saw this one from @rickbeato1 I had to try it. EDIT: A bunch of you asked for tabs…DM me and I’ll send it to you. 

My first attempt was slow and clunky, to say the least. After running it a bunch of times, it came together.

This is the result of some focused repetition, not hours of practice. That’s all it usually takes to add something new to your playing.

And you can’t go wrong with Rick Beato. A master instructor who makes great content for players who want t
